rationale: The page discusses the constitutional validity of narco-analysis tests, citing the Selvi v. State of Karnataka case and Articles 20(3) and 21. It also includes a summary of the SC ruling in PUCL v. Union of India regarding phone tapping and the Telegraph Act.
cleanedText: Exception for Press: Press messages accredited to Central or State Government shall not be intercepted unless their transmission has been prohibited. - Orders issued only by Secretary, Union Ministry of Home Affairs or his State Counterpart in writing; only then tapping can begin. - Exceptional cases: Order may be issued by lower authorities also. Such order has to be communicated to competent authority within a specified time period. - Information Technology Act, 2000: Governs interception of WhatsApp messages, emails, etc. SC Ruling in PUCL v. Union of India (1997): - Upheld the constitutional validity of Section 5(2) of the Telegraph Act. - Laid down procedural safeguards for its application. - Phone tapping orders can only be issued by the home secretary of the state and central governments. - The order, unless it is renewed, shall cease to have authority at the end of two months. 3.4. CONSTITUTIONAL VALIDITY OF NARCO TESTS. Why in the news? Recently, Supreme Court overturned Patna High Court’s order which allowed narco-tests on all accused and witnesses. About Narco-Analysis Test: - Interrogation technique in which a suspect is administered a psychoactive drug to suppress reasoning and self-control. - Drug used is sodium pentothal, which is also used in higher dosages for inducing general anesthesia in surgeries. Key Highlights of Judgement: Judgment draws from Selvi V. State of Karnataka (2010), which examined constitutional validity of narco-analysis and polygraph tests. - Involuntary Narco Test Contravenes Constitution: Such tests violate Articles 20(3) (right against self-incrimination) and 21 (right to personal liberty). Forced narco-tests are illegal and information obtained from involuntary test is inadmissible in court. - Voluntary Narco-Test not sufficient for Conviction: Even if done voluntarily, narco-analysis reports cannot be used directly as evidence. However, facts discovered may be admissible under Indian Evidence Act, 1872. This has also been reiterated by Supreme Court in Vinobhai v. State of Kerala and Manoj Kumar Soni v. State of M.P. cases. - Limited Right to Voluntarily Narco-Test: Accused does not have an absolute right to undergo narco-analysis. It may be permitted voluntarily at an appropriate stage i.e., when accused is exercising his right to lead evidence in a trial.
